text: The surgeon performs a robotic sleeve gastrectomy with hiatal hernia repair. Trocar placement is visible, with one 12mm, three 8mm, and a 5mm incision for a Nathanson retractor to elevate the liver. The robotic vessel sealer isolates and cuts the stomach's blood supply from the greater curvature, minimizing bleeding. The dissection progresses towards the spleen and the angle of His, carefully preserving necessary tissue. A 4cm hiatal hernia is identified and repaired, with visualization of the right and left crura and the inferior vena cava beneath the liver.

The surgeon performs a robotic sleeve gastrectomy, starting with trocar placement below the rib edge. A 12mm trocar, three 8mm trocars, and a 5mm incision for a Nathanson retractor are used to elevate the liver. The anatomy is identified, and the blood supply of the stomach is separated from the greater curvature using a robotic vessel sealer, minimizing bleeding. The procedure progresses towards the spleen and the angle of His, with careful tissue manipulation.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the esophagus from adhesions at the diaphragmatic crura, visualizing the right and left crura. The inferior vena cava is visible beneath the liver on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the esophagus from adhesions at the diaphragmatic cruce, using a 36 French Visigi bougie for calibration and suction. The right and left crura are identified, and the diaphragmatic hernia repair begins with robotic suturing using number one Ethabon sutures to approximate the crural fibers. Care is taken to preserve the vagal nerves and avoid excessive tension on the tissue, ensuring the repair is not too tight to prevent postoperative dysphagia.

The surgeon has placed a 36 French Visigi bougie into the patient, which provides suction to the intragastric space for clear visualization of the staple line and esophagus. The right and left crura are identified, and the surgeon begins repairing the diaphragmatic hernia using a robotic approach, carefully preserving the vagal nerves while approximating the tissue with a number one Ethabon suture.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to re-approximate the crural fibers with sutures, carefully avoiding excessive tension to prevent tearing. The sutures are placed approximately seven to eight millimeters apart, ensuring gentle handling of the tissue near the vagus nerve and inferior vena cava. The robotic tools provide precise control, aiding in the repair of the diaphragmatic hernia while maintaining awareness of surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon tightens sutures using a large needle driver, ensuring the hiatus is closed with adequate tension, approximating the left and right crura. The vessel sealer is used for cutting, minimizing instrument use. The surgeon checks for excess fat or herniated omentum before starting the stapling process. The stomach's color is assessed for viability, and stapling begins four centimeters from the pylorus using a robotic green stapler, guided by a bougie for calibration. The SureForm stapling device is used to ensure a hemostatic staple line, with careful manipulation towards the angle of His.

The surgeon tightens sutures using a large needle driver, achieving an adequate repair of the hiatus by approximating the left and right crura. The vessel sealer is used to cut the sutures, minimizing instrument use. The hiatus is closed, and the surgeon inspects the area for excess fat or herniated omentum before beginning the stapling process. The stomach's color is checked to ensure it is healthy and viable.

The surgeon uses a bougie to calibrate the stapling process, starting approximately four centimeters from the pylorus with a robotic green stapler. The SureForm stapling device is employed to ensure adequate tissue sensing and feedback, creating a hemostatic staple line. The stomach is positioned flat without extra tension, and the stapler is manipulated with precision towards the angle of His, where careful attention is given to prevent leaks.

The surgeon performs a tension-free stapling at the angle of His, ensuring a smooth staple line without excessive tension or irregularity. The stomach is left as a well-uniform cylinder, minimizing post-operative dysphagia. An intraoperative endoscopy is conducted to check for bleeding and confirm the viability of the stomach. A fibrin hemostatic agent is applied to control bleeding, and the stomach is positioned to maintain a smooth angle. The stomach is removed through a twelve millimeter incision and closed with a Carter Thompson device.

The surgeon ensures the gastric sleeve is formed with a smooth staple line, avoiding excessive tension and irregularities. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to check for bleeding and confirm the stomach is a healthy cylindrical tube without torsion. A fibrin hemostatic agent is applied to control bleeding, and a portion of omentum is placed over the stomach. The stomach is removed through a 12mm incision, which is closed using a Carter Thompson device.
